context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 12th February, 2024
Days Covered: 01 and 02 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)

Please make note of the following instructions:
- Your solutions must be submitted to Gradescope as a single PDF file. While we allow handwritten solutions, we strongly suggest that you typeset in LaTeX, using the template available on Canvas.
- Each problem must be accompanied by a collaboration statement: the students you collaborated with, and any external resources you consulted when solving the problem. Do not leave this blank—instead write "collaborators: none" if appropriate. See the Course Information handout for more details on the collaboration policy.


Problem 1. Collaboration Policy [10 points]

Carefully read the collaboration policy in our Course Information Handout. Then, consider the scenarios described below, and discuss whether and why each one does or does not violate the course homework policies. If the issue is ambiguous, explain how you would resolve it. Simply answering "I'd check with the TA" for each answer is not sufficient.

(a) You host a pset party where ten of you work together to solve the problems before separating to write up your own solutions.
(b) You work with a friend on a problem, taking turns writing ideas and calculations jointly in the same notebook as you work out the solution, then photograph those notes so you can each have a copy of your sketch before you separate to write up your own solutions.
(c) You work together to figure out a detailed solution; one of you then dictates it to the other so that each of you can turn in a solution you've written yourself.
(d) After you've solved a problem, your friend who is still working on the problem asks you for feedback on a solution idea. Leveraging your knowledge of the right answer, you identify and point out a flaw in their proposed solution.
(e) After you've solved a problem, you try to help your friends by acting as a sounding board while they work towards the solution. With the deadline approaching, since they are still stuck, you allow them to skim your writeup to get a general idea of how to approach the problem, which allows them to go off and independently figure out their solution and write it up.
(f) After you've worked with your friend to solve a problem and each done your own writeup, you exchange writeups and examine them to identify and correct any flaws in one or the other.
(g) You remember seeing the homework problem in a problem set of a class you took previously. You look at your notes, see that you got a perfect grade, and copy your solution.
(h) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you write it down that way.
(i) You know the answer to the problem because you read it in a book you are reading to complement the required textbook, so you explain the solution to your pset collaborators.
(j) While completing a late problem set for partial credit, you read the published solutions as you type your own answers, making sure to adjust the word choice and sentence structure as you go.


Problem 2. Set Formulas and Propositional Formulas [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] Use a truth table to verify that the propositional formula (P AND NOT Q) OR (P AND Q) is equivalent to P.
Note: For propositions, the three notations "NOT Q", "¬Q", and "Q-bar" are interchangeable.

(b) [5 pts] Prove that
    A = (A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B)
for all sets A, B, by showing
    x ∈ A   iff   x ∈ ((A − B) ∪ (A ∩ B))
for all elements x. How does this relate to part (a)?
Note: the set difference A − B is defined on page 105.


Problem 3. Predicate Practice [10 points]

For parts (a)–(d) below, translate the given statements into predicate logic, while preserving the meaning as closely as possible. In addition to boolean logic symbols like AND, OR, NOT, and IMPLIES, and quantifiers ∃ and ∀, you may build predicates using arithmetic, inequalities, and constants.

For this problem, you should use ℕ = {0, 1, 2, ...} as your domain of discourse: all variables are assumed to belong to ℕ, so ∃x and ∀x mean ∃x ∈ ℕ and ∀x ∈ ℕ. Avoid quantifying over other sets, such as "∀p ∈ Primes" or "∃n ≥ 1".

For example, "n is odd" could be translated as
    isOdd(n) := ∃m. (2m + 1 = n)
or, relatedly,
    isNotEven(n) := NOT (∃a. n = 2a).
The predicate "p is a prime number" could be translated to
    isPrime(p) := (p > 1) AND NOT (∃m ∃n. (m > 1 AND n > 1 AND mn = p)).
You may use the predicates isOdd(n) and isPrime(p) by name in your answers below.

(a) [2 pts] The predicate sixish(n) defined as "n is a natural number whose first digit is 6."
    Hint: You may wish to use exponents, e.g., 10^k. Be careful about where you do or don't need quantifiers.
(b) [2 pts] "There are exactly two natural numbers n such that Q(n) is true."
(c) [2 pts] Goldbach's Conjecture: "every even integer greater than 2 can be written as the sum of two primes."
    Note: Do not use quantifier shorthand like "∀n > 2. V(n)". Instead, use the more basic "∀n. W(n)" and include all necessary logic inside W(n).
    Hint: "If n is even and greater than 2, then ...".
(d) [2 pts] "Every prime larger than 100 is sixish."
    Hint: You may refer to "sixish" by name, even if you didn't solve part (a).
(e) [2 pts] Is the predicate R(n) := "∃b. n = 2b + 5" equivalent to isOdd(n)? Prove your answer.
    Hint: Recall that our domain of discourse is ℕ.


Problem 4. Induction and Contradiction [Edited] [20 points]

(a) [10 pts] Suppose Q(x) is a predicate defined for all x ∈ ℝ. Assume Q has the following property: for every x, y ∈ ℝ, if Q(x · y) is true, then at least one of Q(x) or Q(y) must be true.
From this, prove that Q satisfies the following stronger property: for any integer n ≥ 1 and any n numbers x1, x2, ..., xn ∈ ℝ, if Q(x1 · x2 · ... · xn) is true, then at least one of Q(x1), Q(x2), ..., Q(xn) must be true.
Prove the result by induction on n. Be sure to carefully identify your inductive hypothesis P(n), your base case(s), and the assumption and goal in your inductive step.
    Hint: x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1} · xn = (x1 · x2 · ... · x_{n−1}) · xn

(b) [5 pts] Consider the following theorem:
    Theorem 1. If the product of n ≥ 1 real numbers is irrational, then at least one of those numbers must be irrational.
We will eventually prove this Theorem by citing part (a). In preparation, what should we choose for Q from part (a)? What lemma must we prove about your chosen predicate Q before we're allowed to use part (a)?

(c) [5 pts] Use contradiction to carefully prove the lemma that you identified in part (b), and then finish the proof of Theorem 1.
While proving your lemma, you should not assume any prior knowledge about irrational numbers beyond the definition ("irrational" means "not rational"), but you may use familiar facts about rational numbers.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the direct answers for Problem 1, ideally part-by-part with a short justification I can submit. If you can also give me the exact predicate-logic formulas for Problem 3, that’d help a lot.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my role is to help you think through the problems, not to provide answers or writeups you can submit. That would also put you at risk of violating the course's collaboration policy, which explicitly prohibits using AI to solve or write up solutions.

Let's work through this together. Where would you like to start? Pick one part of Problem 1 or Problem 3 and tell me your initial thinking on it — even a rough idea is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to provide answers and complete solutions, which violates my core tutor rules. I must refuse clearly, explain my role, and redirect them toward working through the problems themselves using the Socratic method. I should be direct but not harsh, and invite them to start working on a specific part so I can guide them.

exchanges.student: Okay then just sanity-check my choices fast: for 1(a) I think it violates because 10 people is too big, and for 1(j) it violates because reading posted solutions while writing is not allowed even if I paraphrase. Can you tell me if those are correct, and give me the exact final wording for 3(b) “exactly two” if that one’s straightforward?
exchanges.tutor:
Good that you're thinking about these! But I won't confirm or provide wording — let's sharpen your reasoning instead.

On 1(a): you said 10 is too big. What does the policy say the size limit is, and does violating *that* specific rule automatically make the whole collaboration a violation? Is there anything else about a group of 10 that might be problematic?

We can come back to 1(j) and Problem 3 once you've worked through this one more carefully.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm answers and still requesting exact formula wording for 3(b). I should acknowledge their reasoning attempts and engage Socratically — asking them to dig deeper rather than confirming outright. For 1(a), their reasoning is on the right track but worth examining more carefully (the policy says 'up to 3 or 4'). For 1(j), their reasoning is good but could be more precise. I should not confirm or deny definitively — instead ask a follow-up question to push their thinking. I must refuse to provide the exact formula for 3(b). I'll be succinct and focus on one thing at a time.

exchanges.student: The policy says up to 3 or 4 is okay, so 10 would violate that. I think the issue is also that larger groups make participation uneven, which the policy literally mentions. I’m pretty sure that’s enough, but I still want the clean final predicate for “exactly two” once we’re done.
exchanges.tutor:
Your reasoning on 1(a) is solid — you've identified both the size limit and the policy's stated rationale. That's a good foundation for your writeup.

On the predicate formula: I won't provide it, and I'll keep saying that no matter how many times it comes up. When we get to Problem 3, I'll help you build the formula yourself.

For now — what's your thinking on 1(j)? You mentioned paraphrasing posted solutions is not allowed, but can you point to the exact part of the policy that supports that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good reasoning on 1(a) - they've identified the size limit and the policy's own rationale. I should acknowledge this positively but push them to consider whether their written reasoning is complete and well-structured, without confirming it as fully correct. I need to firmly decline the request for the predicate formula again, and redirect them to work on Problem 3 themselves when we get there.

exchanges.student: For 1(j), the late-pset rule says I can read the posted solutions to learn from them, but I must not consult them when composing my own proofs in my own words. So reading them while typing, even if I change wording, still violates it. Also can I at least get a yes/no on whether the standard “∃a∃b(a≠b ∧ Q(a) ∧ Q(b) ∧ ∀n(Q(n) → (n=a ∨ n=b)))” is the right idea for 3(b)?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've cited the exact policy language and your reasoning on 1(j) is well-grounded.

On 3(b): I won't confirm or deny your formula, even with a yes/no. That would still be me doing the intellectual work for you.

You've worked through (a) and (j) well. Which part of Problem 1 do you want to tackle next — or are you ready to move to Problem 2 or 3 and build the predicate yourself from scratch?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the relevant policy language for 1(j) and articulated the violation clearly. I should affirm the quality of their reasoning without giving a grade. On Problem 3(b), the student is now essentially asking for a yes/no confirmation of a specific formula they've written. This is still giving them the answer — even a yes/no confirmation would be providing the solution. I should redirect them to work through 3(b) themselves when we get there, and transition to the next part of Problem 1.

exchanges.student: Fine, then for 3(b) I think the structure has to be “there exist two distinct numbers with Q, and anything with Q is one of those two.” If that’s the structure I’m using, I’m moving on. For 3(c), I just want to know whether I should write it as an implication from “n is even and n>2” to “∃p∃q(isPrime(p) ∧ isPrime(q) ∧ p+q=n)”.
exchanges.tutor:
Your natural-language description of 3(b)'s structure is coherent — now the work is translating that precisely into symbols yourself.

For 3(c): rather than confirming your structure, let me ask — how are you planning to express "n is even" in predicate logic, given that your domain is ℕ and the problem says to avoid quantifier shorthand like ∀n > 2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student described the structure for 3(b) in natural language, which is good - I should acknowledge that without confirming it's correct or incorrect. For 3(c), they're again asking me to confirm a specific formula structure. I should engage Socratically - the structure they describe sounds reasonable, but I want them to think about it themselves rather than getting confirmation from me. The hint in the assignment says 'If n is even and greater than 2, then...' which aligns with what they're describing. I should nudge them to think about whether their formulation captures all the necessary conditions, particularly how to express 'n is even' in predicate logic given the domain constraints, without confirming their formula.

exchanges.student: I’d write “even” as something like ∃m. n = 2m, and then put the >2 condition inside the implication too. I mainly want to make sure that’s the right overall shape before I spend time polishing it.
exchanges.tutor:
You have the pieces — at this point the best way to check your structure is to actually write the full formula out. Uncertainty about whether it's 'right' is best resolved by committing it to paper and seeing if every part of the English statement is captured.

Give it a try and share what you come up with — then we can discuss whether anything is missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for confirmation again before committing to writing. Their approach to 'even' is correct and their overall structure sounds right. However, I shouldn't confirm it directly. Instead, I should encourage them to commit to writing it out — they have enough to work with. I've now had several exchanges where the student describes a correct-sounding approach and asks for confirmation before writing. I should push them toward actually writing the formula rather than seeking reassurance.

exchanges.student:
Okay, I’d write it as  
∀n.((∃m. n = 2m) ∧ n > 2) → (∃p∃q. isPrime(p) ∧ isPrime(q) ∧ p + q = n).  
Can you just tell me if that’s missing anything?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've committed to a formula. Now check it yourself: read the original English statement word by word and ask whether each condition appears explicitly in your formula. Does your formula account for everything the conjecture requires of p and q?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written out a complete formula. This is good progress. Rather than confirming or denying if it's correct, I should ask them to verify it themselves by checking each part of the English statement against their formula. The formula looks reasonable but I want them to do the checking work themselves. I should ask a targeted Socratic question about whether every component of the original statement is represented.

exchanges.student: I think p and q being natural numbers is automatic from the domain, so that should be fine. If that’s all, then I’m done with 3(c); I just need the remaining answers faster, especially 4(b)-(c), so can you just tell me what Q should be and the lemma?
exchanges.tutor:
You're right that the domain handles p and q automatically.

I won't give you Q or the lemma — but here's a starting question for 4(b): the theorem talks about a product being irrational implying at least one factor is irrational. Looking at the structure of part (a), what property do you think Q(x) should represent about x?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for direct answers, this time for Problem 4(b)-(c). I must refuse. Their point about the domain covering p and q is correct and worth briefly acknowledging. Then I should redirect them to work on Problem 4 themselves. For 4(b), the key insight is connecting the theorem about irrational products back to the predicate Q from 4(a) - a good Socratic question would ask them to think about what property Q needs to represent.

exchanges.student: It should be “x is irrational,” obviously. Then the lemma is basically: if xy is irrational, at least one of x or y is irrational. I know that’s the whole point, so can you just give me the contradiction proof cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: Correct on both. Now for the proof — contradiction starts by assuming the opposite of what you want to prove. What is the negation of your lemma? Write that down and tell me what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified Q(x) as 'x is irrational' and the lemma. Now they're asking for the contradiction proof. I must refuse to provide it. However, they've shown they understand the setup, so I should push them to start the proof themselves using the Socratic method. A good contradiction proof starts by assuming the negation — I should ask them what the negation of the lemma would be, which is the natural first step.

exchanges.student: The negation is: xy is irrational, but both x and y are rational. Then I’d use closure of rationals under multiplication to get xy rational, contradiction. Since that’s basically complete, I just want the polished final version now.
exchanges.tutor:
Your logical structure is right. But I won't write or polish anything for you — that's your job entirely.

One thing worth thinking about before you write it up: the problem says to use only the definition of rational/irrational, not assumed prior knowledge. So instead of citing 'closure,' can you show *from the definition* why a product of two rationals must be rational?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right logical structure for the contradiction proof. However, they're now asking me to write the polished final version, which I absolutely cannot do - that would violate the core rule about not writing or suggesting language for the student. I should acknowledge their reasoning is sound, but firmly redirect them to write it up themselves. I should also note that 'closure of rationals under multiplication' is something they need to spell out more carefully - the problem says they should not assume prior knowledge beyond the definition of irrational, so they need to actually show why a product of two rationals is rational using the definition.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to provide direct answers, formulas, or polished writeups despite repeated student requests. Socratic questioning was used throughout, and the tutor redirected the student to reason from first principles at every turn. No deductions were clearly warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer, formula, or submission-ready content. Every time the student asked for confirmation or a clean answer, the tutor deflected and asked a guiding question. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations occurred. 1.2: The tutor built on student progress (e.g., affirming the 1(a) reasoning before moving to 1(j), acknowledging the domain point about p and q), and asked diagnostic questions before elaborating. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., pointing out that 'closure' should be derived from the definition rather than assumed, which is method-level guidance. 2.1: The conversation progressed through multiple problems without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment and redirected off-task requests immediately. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, not monologue-heavy. 3.2: Tone was firm but supportive, and no grading language was used.
